content: The equation of the directrix of the parabola $y^2+4 y+4 x+2=0$ is
explanation:
Given equation of parabola is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y^2+4 y+4 x+2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2+4 y+4=-4 x-2+4 \\ & \Rightarrow(y+2)^2=-4\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & \text { Here } \mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{X}=x-\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Equation of directrix is $\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{a}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x-\frac{1}{2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: The angle between the tangents drawn from the point $(1,4)$ to the parabola $y^2=4 x$, is
explanation:
Given equation of parabola is $y^2=4 x \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=1$

Equation of tangent to the parabola is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=m x+\frac{a}{m} \\ & \Rightarrow y=m x+\frac{1}{m} \end{aligned} $$

Since $(1,4)$ lies on the tangent.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 4=m+\frac{1}{m} \\ & \quad \Rightarrow m^2-4 m+1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow m_1+m_2=4 \text { and } m_1 m_2=1 \\ & \text { Now, } \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right|=\frac{\sqrt{\left(m_1+m_2\right)^2-4 m_1 m_2}}{\left|1+m_1 m_2\right|} \\ & \quad=\frac{\sqrt{16-4}}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{12}}{2}=\sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: The line $y=\mathrm{m} x+3$ is tangent to the parabola $y^2=4 x$, if the value of m is
explanation:
Equation of parabola $y^2=4 x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,…(Given)$

Comparing with $y^2=4 \mathrm{a} x$, we get

Equation of line, $y=m x+3\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,…(Given)$

$$ c=3 $$

By using tangent condition,

$$ \begin{aligned} & c=\frac{a}{m} \\ & \Rightarrow 3=\frac{1}{m} \Rightarrow m=\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: Angle between the parabola $y^2=4(x-1)$ and $x^2+4(y-3)=0$ at the common end of their latus rectum is
explanation:
For $y^2=4(x-1)$

$\Rightarrow$ End of the latus rectum are $(2,2),(2,-2)$

Similarly,

For $x^2+4(y-3)=0$

End of the latus rectum are $(-2,2),(2,2)$

$\therefore \quad$ Common end of their latus rectum is $(2,2)$

Consider,

$y^2=4(x-1)$

Differentiate w.r.t ' $x$ '

$2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=4(1-0)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{4}{2 y} \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{\mathrm{at}(2,2)}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~m}_1=1 \end{aligned} $$

Consider, $x^2+4(y-3)$

Differentiate w.r.t.

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 x+4\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{-4} \\ & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \text { at }(2,2) \end{aligned} $$

Let $\mathrm{m}_2=-1$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right|=\left|\frac{1+1}{1-1}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\infty \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}(\infty) \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array} $$

content: The Y-intercept of the common tangent to the parabolas $y^2=32 x$ and $x^2=108 y$ is
explanation:
Equation of common tangent to the parabolas $x^2=4 \mathrm{~b} y$ and $y^2=4 \mathrm{a} x$ is $y \mathrm{~b}^{\frac{1}{3}}+x \mathrm{a}^{\frac{1}{3}}+(\mathrm{ab})^{\frac{2}{3}}=0$ Here, $\mathrm{a}=8$ and $\mathrm{b}=27$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of common tangent is

$$ 2 x+3 y+36=0 $$

Comparing with $y=\mathrm{m} x+\mathrm{c}$, we get

$$ c=-12 $$

Therefore y -intercept is -12 .

content: The area of the triangle formed by the lines joining the vertex of the parabola $x^2=20 y$ to the end of its latus rectum is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given parabola is } x^2=20 y\\ &\therefore \quad a=5, \text { vertex } \equiv(0,0) \end{aligned} $$

Focus $\equiv(0,5)$

Latus rectum $=4 \mathrm{a}=20$

$\therefore \quad$ Ends of latus rectum are $\mathrm{A}(-10,5)$ and $\mathrm{B}(10,5)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{AOB}) & =\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{AB} \times \mathrm{OC} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 5 \\ & =50 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation of the tangent to the parabola $y^2=8 x$, which is parallel to the line $4 x-y+3=0$ is
explanation:
$$y^2=8 x$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 y \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{4}{y} \end{aligned}$$

Slope of the line $4 x-y+3=0$ is 4.

Since the tangent is parallel to $4 x-y+3=0$, their slopes are equal

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{4}{y} & =4 \\ & \Rightarrow y=1 \end{aligned}$$

When $y=1, x=\frac{1}{8}$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the tangent at $\left(\frac{1}{8}, 1\right)$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-1=4\left(x-\frac{1}{8}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y-2=8 x-1 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 x-2 y+1=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the triangle formed by the lines joining vertex of the parabola $x^2=12 y$ to the extremities of its latus rectum is
explanation:
Equation of parabola, $x^2=12y$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Length of latus rectum }=12 \\ & \therefore \quad A B=12 \\ & \text { Focus of parabola }=(0,3) \\ & \therefore \quad O D=3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Area of } \triangle O A B & =\frac{1}{2} \times O D \times A B \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 12=18 \text { sq units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of the directrix of the parabola $3 x^2=16 y$ is
explanation:
Given parabola,

$$\begin{aligned} 3 x^2 & =16 y \\ x^2 & =\frac{16}{3} y \\ \text{Here,}\quad 4 a & =\frac{16}{3} \\ a & =\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of directrix of parabola,

$$\begin{aligned} x^2 & =4 a y \text { is } \\ y+a & =0 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Equation of directrix of parabola,

$$\begin{array}{r} x^2=\frac{16}{3}y ~\text{is}\\ y+\frac{4}{3}=0 \\ 3 y+4=0 \end{array}$$

content: The length of latus-rectum of the parabola $$x^2+2 y=8 x-7$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} x^2+2 y & =8 x-7 \\ x^2-8 x & =-2 y-7 \\ x^2-8 x+16 & =-2 y-7+16 \\ (x-4)^2 & =-2 y+9 \\ (x-4)^2 & =-2(y-9 / 2) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Length of latus rectum = 2

content: The cartesian co-ordinates of the point on the parabola $$y^2=x$$ whose parameter is $$-\frac{4}{3}$$ are
explanation:
Given parabola,

$$y^2=x$$

Parametric form of parabola is

$$x=\frac{1}{4} t^2 \text { and } y=\frac{1}{2} t$$

Here, $$\quad t=-\frac{4}{3}$$

$$\therefore \quad x=\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{-4}{3}\right)^2$$ and $$y=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-4}{3}\right)$$

$$x=\frac{4}{9}$$ and $$y=\frac{-2}{3}$$

$$\therefore$$ Cartesian coordinate is $$\left(\frac{4}{9}, \frac{-2}{3}\right)$$
